CampNote: GG: This was a moving camp. It occupied the Channel Island Alderney from 03/05/1943 to 06/25/1944, and then was moved to a different location (eventually Kortemark, Belgium, by way of St. Malo and a number of other northern French towns).
DemogNote: ED: selected men. GG: "The SS-Baubrigaden consisted as a rule of male non-Jewish prisoners… German prisoners usually formed the thin layer of leaders as prisoner-functionaries, while the large majority of the prisoners were of Polish or Soviet nationality." (p. 1355, main essay on SS-Baubrigaden and SS-Eisenbahnbaubrigaden)
EncyTitle: Early Camps, Youth Camps, and Concentration Camps and Subcamps under the SS-Business Administration Office (WVHA)
EpidemNote: ED: selected unspecified epidemic, as entry states that deaths can be ascribed to epidemics
HPNote: GG: Holding places were noted as barracks, but when SS-BB I arrived they were not completed yet, so "many prisoners had to live outside for a longer time." (p. 1361) The camp was "located on the grounds of the former British airfield" (p. 1361) and had been "constructed in August 1942 for workers from the East" (p. 1361) so HPReuse category as well as HPNew (mention of newly built barracks as well) both selected. MK: HoldPStruc: other=field.
Incomers: Sachsenhausen via Düsseldorf; Neungamme via Düsseldorf
InmateNote: GG: The original population of SS-BB I was made up of prisoners from Sachsenhausen (730) and Neuengamme (270).
LExNote: GG: Add SS-Baubrigade as its own LEx.
LabPlNote: GG: "They were almost exclusively employed in heavy physical labor, building tunnels or roads, and excavating material used for construction purposes. When they worked on building fortifications during 1943, their work was supervised directly by OT companies." (p. 1361)
LaborNote: GG: "They were almost exclusively employed in heavy physical labor, building tunnels or roads, and excavating material used for construction purposes. When they worked on building fortifications during 1943, their work was supervised directly by OT companies." (p. 1361) ED: LabortType: selected camp operations, as Kapos terrorized prisoners
LaborType: building material;camp construction;camp operations;civic defense;construction;SS-assigned labor
MoveNote: GG: The very nature of Alderney being occupied by SS-BB I makes it an incoming and outgoing camp. The 1000 prisoners of SS-BB I came to the island 03/05/1943. Prior to SS-BB I, the camp is noted as having been "constructed in August 1942 for workers from the East" prior to the arrival of SS-BB I. (p. 1361)  Outgoing: "In June 1943, 200 prisoners were classified as unfit for work, and on instruction from the military commander of the island, List ordered that they be returned to the Neuengamme concentration camp." (p. 1361)  "In early July 1943, about 150 prisoners were taken to Cherbourg." "At the end of 1943, the construction brigade was assigned to help build launching ramps for the V-2 rocket at St. Omer and, to that end, was transferred on December 17, 1943, to the “Rommel Barracks” in Cherbourg on the French mainland. However, Dorsch successfully intervened with Himmler, and the construction brigade was transferred back to Alderney on January 7, 1944." (p. 1362)  Remove last: June 24-25 1944 to Kortemark, viaSt. Malo,  Rennes, Nantes, Angers, Tours, Dijon, Nancy, Toul, Namur, and Ghent.
PMaxNote: GG: Encyclopedia lists the original number of prisoners, 1000. It also notes that by the time SS-BB I left the island, there were only 636 prisoners left. ED: Nation: selected Russian, German
RemoveLast: St. Malo to Rennes to Nantes to Angers to Tours to Dijon to Nancy to Toul to Namur to Ghent to Kortemark.

LaborNote: GG: The labor in the camp was primarily construction or the transport of construction materials around the site. It is also worth noting that Ferdinand Porsche was heavilly involved in the initial plans to construct this camp, see the encyclopedia entry for more detailed information on his involvement. ED: added camp operations, since prisoner functionaries oversaw prisoners
PMaxNote: GG :"The number of prisoners probably never exceeded 500, but quantitative information on this camp is scarce, and witness testimony extremely contradictory."

However, the offi ce of the Senator for Construction (Senator für Bauwesen) noted that as the camp “did not meet the necessary requirements for guarding concentration camp prisoners,” a different site had to be found. A comparison with other photos from the early days of construction of the Lias factory suggests that it was only the concentration camp area that was fenced in with barbed wire. … The prisoners worked daily for 11 hours. The only mention of its existence is to be found in a special order of the Natzweiler  concentration camp, dated September 26, 1944, dealing with  the assignments of the companies of guards. Rebuilt, it was opened in October 1939 as a work education camp (Arbeitserziehungslager) and police detention camp (Polizeihaftlager) as well as an SS special camp (Sonderlager)." LExNote: GG: LExName: It is worth noting that the camp was originally built in 1938 by the DAF as an OT forced labor/workers camp. It then burned down, and was rebuilt in October 1939 as an Arbeitserziehungslager, Polizeihaftlager, and Sonderlager. The camp reported to the "Inspector of the Security Police and SD, who also was the leader of the Security Staff (Sicherungsstab) at the OT." (p. 823) In 1942, the administration of Hinzert changed to the WVHA, but also had its own Gestapo interrogation squad as well.
LaborNote: GG: "About 10 percent of the inmates worked in the internal prisoner detachments inside the camp itself. HPNote: JH: "The location of the subcamp cannot be exactly determined. The witness statements contradict each other. Several reports state that the prisoners were accommodated in part of the barracks on the Schafswiese. What is more likely is that the camp was located above the paper mill in an empty twostory store house of the former hotel Talbrauerei. The prisoners were housed on the fi rst fl oor, while the guards’ accommodation was on the ground fl oor."
InmateNote: JH: InmateType: "Hungarian and Polish Jews… non- Jewish prisoners from the Soviet Union, Poland, and France."
LaborNote: JH: It is not clearly stated where the camp inmates worked. The entry lists previous work detachments that commuted to Ilfeld and were then transferred to the Ilfeld camp, but it is not clear which work detachments still existed. "During the course of 1944, other prisoner detachments from the camps at Dora and Harzungen were forced to expand the Mittelwerk infrastructure in several locations in Ilfeld, including the laying of water pipes in the main street (Adolf- Hitler- Allee), the construction of air- raid shelters on the eastern edge of the village (Herzberg), and the construction of a waterworks (Kommando Rosensteg). There were to be other labor detachments, especially in Mittelwerk’s tailor shop based in the Ilfeld Kloster school, which since the autumn of 1943 had been the seat of the Mittelwerk GmbH administration, and the Günther paper mill, where prisoners worked for the Mittelwerk in establishing an assembly hall for the “Volksjäger” (People’s Fighter) He 162."
MoveNote: JH: RemoveLast: One stop in between Niedersachserfen and the killing sites was either Walkenried or Osterode. "the prisoners were driven by their guards on foot to Niedersachswerfen and from there in a northwesterly direction. In Walkenried or Osterode in the Harz Mountains, the survivors of the march joined a railway transport of prisoners from the Rottleberode camp. InmateNote: MK: InmateType: other = officers and noncommissioned officers (NCOs) who were unable to work. Various = virtually all nations of Europe.
LExNote: MK: the subcamp chapter says, "The Granitwerke Mauthausen, (Mauthausen Granite Works) owned by the German Earth and Stone Works (DESt) was established in St. Georgen an der Gusen. Its purpose was to exploit the quarrries. Even before the Mauthausen concentration camp was opened on August 8, 1938, the DESt had leased the quarries at Mauthausen and Gusen. It would later assume complete ownership." So I selected DESt and Mauthausen Granite Works must be added.
LaborNote: MK: WorkProx: the quarry seems to have been right outside the camp. JH: LaborType: This makes sex work ery likely: "Block One housed the camp clerk and, beginning in 1943, a brothel." The fact that "prisoners worked in the quarry" and their work included "strenuous manual labor" indicates "building material."
MoveNote: MK: Outgoers: Hartheim was a euthanasia center.
Outgoers: Steyr crematorium/the dead; Mauthausen/Gusen; Hartheim euthanasia center
PMaxNote: JH: The 58,000 only refers to prisoners being newly registered at the camp within the year of 1944, not the actual oocupancy of the camp. The 45,000 in spring 1945 also includes the Gusen subcamp. The Gusen entry states that "On May 4, 1945, that number had swelled to 20,487." So, I selected the number of prisoners upon liberation, "about 21,000," although the actual number was probably higher, possibly up to 24000, but that is only calculation from the 45000 in both camps in spring 1945.

CampNote: JH: We decided to treat Schirmeck-Vorbrück as a separate camp. "The fi rst camp was built next to a small town in the Vosges, Schirmeck, about 50 kilometers (31 miles) from Strasbourg, and received the name of Schirmeck- Vorbrück (French: Schirmeck- La Broque). Some 60 Alsatians who had led anti- German activities before the war or who had deserted the German army during World War I were immediately interned. The camp functioned throughout the entire war but never became a concentration camp. It was more of a local work camp, labeled education camp (Erziehungslager) or detention camp (Sicherungslager)."

LaborType: "Some Kapos behaved particularly brutally and killed dozens of inmates. Positions within the camp’s administration were held by po liti cal prisoners…. An enormous heating installation, the Kesselhaus, was built and fortifi ed to resist bombardments. … The hardest excavation work was reserved for camp inmates. … construction work … One group of inmates worked in the galleries of the mines at Obrigheim and Neckarzimmern; another group was employed in a cement factory, and a third in a pickle plant (Gurkenkonservenfabrik). … built barracks"
MoveNote: JH: Incomers: It is uncertain whether these prisoners stayed in Neckarelz I over night or if this was part of their evacuation march in the same day. "The inmates of the sub- subcamps of Asbach, Bad Rappenau, and Neckarbischofsheim were sent to Neckarelz I."

"In the Neckarelz I camp and the three subsubcamps there were 2,129 inmates in June 1944 (1,100 in Neckarelz alone) and 2,944 in September. In Neckarelz II, there were an average of 1,400 inmates."

Incomers: SS-Baubrigaden/Berlin/SS- BB II; SS-Baubrigaden/Müggelheim/SS-BB II; SS-Baubrigaden/Friedrich- Krause- Ufer/SS-BB II; Berlin-Lichterfelde/Sachsenhausen
InmateNote: ED: Nation: selected various, as an eyewitnesses mentions his "comrades from many nations"
LExNote: GG: Add SS-Eisenbahnbaubrigade as LEx
LaborNote: GG: WorkProx: due to the mobile nature of the camp, the work would have been both distant and near based on the day-to-day location of the camp. In some places, like the Nürnberg railway station, the work was near. In others, it was reported that the prisoners were held overnight at one location and then "traveled" (unclear if by railcar or foot) to another location. (p. 1389)
MoveNote: GG: The dissolution of the Eisenbahnbaubrigade occurred at the end of April, 1945. The encyclopedia reports that some of the prisoners were marched out of Landshut, but does not report how far they got before the SS guards fled and the prisoners were liberated by the Americans. It also reports that some of the prisoners were left in Landeshut, and possibly that another group of prisoners was "taken to Graz" without any additional information. (p. 1389)
PMaxNote: GG: This is the largest number mentioned in the entry, as the starting population of the Eisenbahnbaubrigade as it left Berlin.

LaborNote: GG: LaborType and WorkProx: The labor done by the prisoners in SS-BB III in this region was mostly construction. However, the encyclopedia notes that the prisoners specifically held at the Wieda camp did different labor, often less physically demanding. Because the Wieda camp was the administrative camp for the region, much of the labor was ad hoc construction, administrative work, or miscelaneous work for locals. "Living conditions in this auxiliary camp generally were better than in the subcamps, especially as Wieda had the central kitchen. In addition, inmates here did not have to do exhausting construction work but, instead, either remained in the camp, built barracks in Niedersachswerfen, worked for private persons, or were set to work in or by the communal administration." (p. 1397). ED: deselected other
MoveNote: GG: The encyclopedia describes the journey out of Wieda. The prisoners were taken to Wernigerode, some by foot some by train, where they were then loaded into train cars and traveled by train to Letzlingen. Many escaped there, but some were recaptured and forced to march. The encyclopedia does not give much detail on the locations of the forced marches.
